title: How AI Turned a Museum’s Masterpieces into a 600-Year-Old Mystery
content:
When Argentina’s Museo Nacional de Bellas Artes opened its doors to an algorithm, a beer brand and a question nobody could quite answer, the result was the museum’s most popular interactive experience to date.

Stand in front of Pieter Brueghel’s The Peasant Wedding and you’ll see revellers, jugs, and tankards of something golden being passed around a crowded table. What you can’t know, what nobody can know, is exactly what they’re drinking. According to an algorithm built in Buenos Aires, however, there is a 78% probability it’s a Stella Artois.

That unlikely statistic is the basis of The Artois Probability, a collaboration between Stella Artois, creative agency GUT Buenos Aires and the Museo Nacional de Bellas Artes. The project asked whether data science could answer an art-historical question hiding in plain sight. The beer’s brewing heritage stretches back to Leuven in 1366, so how likely is it that the drinks in Europe’s great paintings, the Manets and Van Goghs and Brueghels, were actually an Artois?
snippet: When Argentina’s Museo Nacional de Bellas Artes opened its doors to an algorithm, a beer brand and a question nobody could quite answer, the result was the museum’s most popular interactive experience
creator: Manual Charr

title: When Students Know More Than Teachers: AI in Museum Learning
content:
When most institutions talk about bringing AI into education, the instinct is to hand students the tools and watch what they make. The Victoria and Albert Museum took the opposite route. Faced with the question of how to bring AI into its learning programmes, the museum’s Schools and Colleges team made a deliberate decision to start with the teachers.

The reasoning, as David Houston, the team’s leader, explained at the Museum AI Summit, came down to a hard look at reality. AI is already in schools whether anyone feels ready for it or not, and what the team kept hearing from teachers was a deeply mixed picture. Some were using it confidently, folding it into lesson planning and classroom practice. Others didn’t have the time or the confidence to engage with it at all. Beneath that sat the problem of access: some students arrive with powerful devices and tools at home, others with almost nothing. Rather than try to recreate that gap inside the museum, the team chose to support the people who have to make sense of all this for a room full of children at a time.
snippet: When most institutions talk about bringing AI into education, the instinct is to hand students the tools and watch what they make. The Victoria and Albert Museum took the opposite route. Faced with
creator: Manual Charr

title: We Sent 400 Visitors on a Date With a Van Gogh. Here’s What We Learned
content:
The Van Gogh Museum built an AI feature that matched people to a painting and told them a personal story about why they belonged together. Visitors loved it. Then the team had a decision to make.

At a recent Museum AI Summit, two people from the Van Gogh Museum in Amsterdam — curator of education Jolein van Kregten and digital education product manager Fenne de Beer — walked through a pilot they had clearly poured themselves into. It’s a good story about building with AI inside a museum. It doesn’t end where you’d expect.

The premise started with a frustration any gallery educator will recognise. People wander through rooms. They glance, they photograph, they move on. They don’t really look. And looking — slowly, with some context — is the thing that turns a painting from wallpaper into something you remember.
snippet: The Van Gogh Museum built an AI feature that matched people to a painting and told them a personal story about why they belonged together. Visitors loved it. Then the team had a decision to make.
creator: Manual Charr

title: Bringing a Museum Collection to Life with Conversational AI
content:
Inside the London Transport Museum’s AI Digital Poster project — a practical case study in using conversational AI to make museum collections speak, in 60 languages, for under £10,000.

What if a museum poster could talk back? What if visitors could ask it anything — in their own language — and get an accurate, curator-approved answer in seconds?

That’s no longer a thought experiment. At the London Transport Museum in Covent Garden, an Art Deco poster from 1927 now holds conversations with visitors as an AI-powered Digital Museum Guide. Launching this June in the Global Poster Gallery, the project is one of the clearest, most replicable examples yet of how conversational AI can widen access to heritage collections without giving away a museum’s most valuable asset: its knowledge.

At the Museum AI Summit 2026, the team behind the project explained how it was built, what it cost, and the lessons any museum can take from it.
snippet: Inside the London Transport Museum’s AI Digital Poster project — a practical case study in using conversational AI to make museum collections speak, in 60 languages, for under £10,000.
creator: Manual Charr
